-- 1 of 5 --

The Seed Launch Part 2
!
Now I want to talk about the offer for your seed launch. First of all, you do not have to
do all this. I've got a laundry list here for you. Just go through here and pick and
choose; you don't have to do it all, so please don't get intimidated. I just gave you this
list for brainstorming purposes. First of all, teleseminars or webinars I see as being a
real key part of the seed launch. You definitely want to survey after each one. Ask
people what questions they are on the topic you had the last time.
!
If you're teaching about guitar, and you did a module or a teleseminar ...which will
probably end up becoming a module ... and it's about how to tune your guitar, then say,
"Hey folks. Our last call was about learning how to tune your guitar. Could you tell me
what questions you still have about tuning the guitar?" You send them to a survey; at
the survey you should tell them what the next topic is going to be, and ask them about
questions they have about that topic.
Say, "Thanks a lot for telling us about what you thought about that last seminar. Our
next one is going to be about playing the D chord. What do I need to cover about
playing the D chord? Please let me know so I can be sure to cover everything that
you're wondering about." Surveys are real key. Sometimes I get a question ... could you
just do this as a video and release a video each week, or as each module? You can
certainly do that. I just think with the seed launch, doing it live interactively is generally
a higher perceived value. Not always, but usually. I would go with the teleseminar or
the webinar.
!
Guest experts. If you don't have the knowledge about something and you want to do
a product about it ... if you want to do a seed launch about it ... then you can just line
up a series of experts and interview them. Or you can add in interviews with people as
a bonus, or to fill out the offer. I told you how I did it with the Product Launch
Workshop. I did a series of webinars ... actually teleseminars ... and then I also had
several interviews. That's a way to round out your offer. These could be live calls, or
they could be recorded. Here I've got a typo that says webinars. They could be live, or
they could be recorded ahead of time with the expert. Generally live is going to have a
higher perceived value.
You could do hot seats. Basically that's where if someone is working on something,
then you could have them turn in their work and then you could go through their work
and give them suggestions. This is a participation type of thing. People love this. If
they're wondering how to write a resume ... if you're teaching how to write a resume ...
they can send you their resume, and you can go through their resume and make
tweaks and show them what you would do differently. That's a hot seat.
You could supplement your offer ... your teleseminars or webinars ... with additional
recorded video or audio that could be bonuses. You could do case studies. The
difference between a case study and a hot seat is a hot seat is before the fact, and a
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 2
PLF_Module1v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 2 of 5 --

case study is after the fact. Someone can show what they did and the results they got,
and then everyone can learn from what they did and their results.
You could do question and answer calls. Usually if I'm doing something like this
during my teleseminars or webinars, I'll be taking questions, but then I'll come back
with a Q and A call afterwards that's just nothing but questions and answers; people
love that. If you want to really build out the value of the offer, you could also offer
additional one on one consulting calls. Everyone who buys, they get a a series of ...
maybe they get 4 or 5 teleseminars, but they also get a 30 minute, or a 20 minute, or a
60 minute call with you, where you can coach them individually. That adds a lot of value
to an offer.
You can give them the recordings of all the calls. In fact, that's something you really
should do, because as I mentioned earlier, not everyone is going to show up for all your
calls or all the webinars so you definitely want to record them. Even if you weren't
going to give them to people, you want the recordings so you can listen and you can
assess how you did.
You can offer the transcripts. Take the recordings and get them transcribed. An hour
transcription might cost you anywhere from $50 to $100 to get transcribed. Then you'll
have to do some editing after you get the transcription back. A lot of people release
transcripts without editing them at all. I personally, being the perfectionist I am, we
spend a bunch of time going through and making them read a lot better. Generally, the
way we speak when it gets transcribed ... it just doesn't read all that well; it doesn't
flow that well. We actually try to turn them into something that's readable.
Again, that's something ... even if you don't release those transcripts ... I like to get
stuff that I record transcribed just so I can look back and see how I did, see how I
taught, see what I said.
!
Then, a really crazy thought is you could do this as a live workshop for much higher
value. If you did that, your seed launch ... we're going to talk about pricing in just a
moment ... the pricing would usually go through the roof if you're going to do it as a
live workshop.
!
If you're going to teach this stuff in a live environment, have people travel to you ..
maybe even if you're doing it in your local environment, have them come to you, be in
a room with you ... and you can teach a group of 5 or 6 people. Or, you could teach a
group of a couple hundred people. But a seed launch ... you're usually going to be
teaching anywhere from ... in a live environment it would be probably 10 to 30 people.
You get much above 30 and you're going to lose some of the interaction.
!
This is a way to go real high value with your seed launch. If you did this, you would
have a bigger list, and then you would be going for a higher price. Most of the time ...
the seed launches ... you're going at a very small list where maybe you just have 50 or
100, or 200 people on the list; then you're doing one of these teleseminar based ones.
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 3
PLF_Module3v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 3 of 5 --

But say you had a business where you had a large list ... maybe 1,000 or 2,000 or a lot
bigger ... and you wanted to come up with a new product, you could do this seed
launch.
Put a smaller group in the workshop. Probably film, or at least do audio recording of the
workshop ... get a much higher price, and that could work very, very effectively. In fact,
that's essentially what I did with my Product Launch Manager Program where I basically
did it as a very high end. We took 50 primary attendees. We allowed them to bring a
business partner, but I think we charged $25,000 for that. I had a list of tens of
thousands of people and I offered this very, very small workshop ... 50 people. By the
time you added in guests, we probably had 60 or 70 people there.
!
We did it as a 5 day workshop. This shows where this idea of the seed launch can go
to. Charged $25,000 for it, sold it out right away because I had the big list, and I had a
hot list. My list loved my stuff; they were very interested in what I've got to do. I had a
very high value offer ... $25,000. Did that event, recorded the video, and then later on
turned it into a home study course. That's how I did a seed launch at a very high
level ... a very high dollar level, and a very impactful level.
Then that home study course went on to sell hundreds of those. That was at a $10,000
offer, and it just drew millions of dollars with that launch. This seed launch ... I'm sort
of portraying it as a way to get started, but you can take the same concept and blow it
out a lot bigger.
Pricing. Pricing for your offer. It really depends on your market. If you're teaching
guitar, it's going to be a lot lower price than if you're teaching salesmen who are selling
medical devices or something like that. It really depends on your market. If you can get
a higher price ... again, high price is relative to your market ... the high price builds
value for the future product.
For instance, with that Product Launch Manager launch I did ... the initial event at
$25,000 ... that established the value going forward. Then I was able to sell the home
study course for a very, very high price. Higher price usually means higher compliance.
By compliance I mean you want people to show up for your teleseminars, and your
webinars, or your event. Whatever you're doing ... you want them to show up . A
higher price usually will motivate them to show up. It will also motivate them to do the
work and get the results.
!
However, the most important thing is to fill the class and get that critical mass.
Remember, you want 30 people ... 30, 40, 50, 60 people. You don't want 5 people,
because if you have 5 people, you're not going to have that many people showing up;
you're not going to have much interaction. If you have to price it lower to get people to
sign up so you can have it be more interactive, then go with the lower pricing.
Now I told you about how I did it with my first Product Launch Workshop. I sold it at a
high price. Like I said, again I can't remember if it was $2,000 or $3,000 ... let's just
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 4
PLF_Module1v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 4 of 5 --

say it was $2,000. It's a high price, but I didn't sell that many, so I comped people; I let
other people in for free. It wasn't like I went out to the world and said, "Hey, you can
get in for free." I just went privately to some of my mastermind partners and some of
my friends who really wanted to know this stuff, and I let them in for free.
They're pretty smart. They realized they got in for free. They realized that other people
paid a lot of money, so they're not going to go and get on the calls and say, "Hey, Jeff.
This is great. Thanks for letting me in free." People are smart that way. Or, the bottom
one here I say you could do this entire thing for free. It's not ideal to do it for free
because of the perceived value, and because of the compliance ... meaning people
aren't going to take you seriously. They're not going to show up; they're not going to do
the homework when you give something away for free.
But, it's a possibility. If you're just starting from scratch and you have no list, or you
have some people that do take your stuff really, really seriously but you don't think you
can get them to pay, you could do it for free. Definitely not my preferred way to do
things. That is the offer for your seed launch.
